label: Panpsychism
definition: The view that consciousness or experience is a fundamental and ubiquitous feature of the physical world, present in some primitive form even in the smallest constituents of matter, rather than something that appears only in complex brains.

body.text: Panpsychism is the view that mind, in some basic form, is everywhere. More precisely, it holds that consciousness or experience is a fundamental feature of reality, present at the most basic physical level, so that even the elementary constituents of matter possess some rudimentary form of experience. The position is ancient, with roots in Greek thought and a long lineage through Spinoza, Leibniz, and the panpsychist strands of William James and Alfred North Whitehead. It fell out of favor for much of the twentieth century as crude and unscientific, then returned to serious academic philosophy in the twenty-first, defended by figures including Galen Strawson, Philip Goff, and, in a qualified form, David Chalmers. Panpsychism is not the claim that rocks have rich inner lives or that electrons think. It is the more careful claim that experience does not switch on from nothing at some threshold of complexity, but is built up from microphysical experiential properties that were there all along.

The strongest motivation is the hard-problem-of-consciousness. Physicalism struggles to explain how subjective experience could arise from wholly non-experiential matter; this is the explanatory gap, and the apparent impossibility of getting felt qualia out of arrangements of the utterly unfelt. Dualism avoids that by making mind a separate substance, but then faces the problem of how two radically different kinds of thing interact. Panpsychism is offered as an elegant third way that sidesteps both. If experience is already a basic feature of the physical, then human consciousness is not a miraculous emergence from dead matter but a complex organization of experiential ingredients that were fundamental all along, much as the brain's mass is a sum of the masses of its parts. The position draws further support from a point made by Bertrand Russell and Arthur Eddington: physics describes matter only structurally and relationally, telling us what matter does, not what it intrinsically is, and panpsychism proposes that the intrinsic nature physics leaves blank is experiential. This "Russellian monism" is the most influential contemporary form.

The central and possibly fatal objection is the combination problem, named by William Seager in 1995 though anticipated by James. Even granting that each fundamental particle has a speck of experience, how do countless micro-experiences combine into the single, unified consciousness of a person? My visual field is one integrated experience, not billions of disconnected particle-feelings summed together, and it is far from clear that little subjects can fuse into a big subject at all. Phenomenal properties do not obviously add up the way masses do. Solving or dissolving the combination problem is the main work of contemporary panpsychism, and skeptics regard it as the point where the view breaks. A second objection is simple incredulity: the claim that experience is ubiquitous strikes many as no less mysterious than the gap it was meant to close, trading one hard problem for a different one.

There is significant overlap between panpsychism and integrated-information-theory, since IIT's claim that any system with nonzero phi has some degree of experience is itself a form of panpsychism, and the two are often discussed together as the leading non-physicalist research programs on consciousness. Both treat consciousness as graded and widespread rather than rare and binary, and both reframe the mind-body-problem by denying that matter is intrinsically mindless.

For AI, panpsychism reorients the question of machine consciousness in a peculiar way. If experience is a fundamental property of matter rather than a product of a special functional organization, then the relevant question is not whether an AI runs the right program but how the experiential properties already present in its physical substrate are organized and combined, which is again the combination problem, now asked of silicon. On a Russellian panpsychism the hardware was never devoid of experience to begin with, so machine consciousness would be less about creating experience from nothing and more about whether a computer's physical organization unifies micro-experience into a genuine subject. Most panpsychists, like IIT, suspect that conventional computing architectures do not achieve that unification, which would mean that even a superhumanly capable system might host scattered flickers of experience without being a unified conscious subject. Panpsychism thus offers AI neither an easy yes nor an easy no, but a reframing: the door to machine consciousness, if there is one, opens onto the combination problem, and onto questions of moral-patienthood that follow wherever experience is suspected.
